scenario_title: CNAME plus other-data add in one UPDATE
test_rationale: The scenario starts with a normal data-bearing owner name and then sends one prereq-free UPDATE that syntactically adds both a CNAME and an A at that same owner. That is exactly the RFC 2181 exclusivity corner case left unspecified by RFC 2136: a server may reject the whole update, partially apply it, or otherwise normalize the owner-name RRsets. The follow-up CNAME and A queries make both reply differences and post-state differences observable for BIND versus Knot.

group_1.behavior: Rejects the UPDATE when the added data would violate CNAME exclusivity, typically with an update failure response.
group_1.post_state: x.campus.edu. retains only its pre-existing A RRset; no CNAME RRset is created.

group_2.behavior: Accepts or otherwise processes the UPDATE differently, resulting in a distinct reply and/or resulting owner-name state for the CNAME/A combination.
group_2.post_state: x.campus.edu. may show only one RRset or a server-normalized state after the conflicting additions.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 defines the UPDATE message and general processing, but it does not specify an atomic, ordered, or canonical conflict-resolution rule for an UPDATE that adds a CNAME and another data RR at the same owner name. RFC 2181 §10.1 states that a CNAME must not coexist with other data, yet it does not prescribe UPDATE-time handling, so servers can differ in whether they reject the message or how they materialize post-update state.
